input_path: data\papers\Ramdhan et al. - 2025 - Engineering Automotive Digital Twins on Standardized Architectures A Case Study.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: A 1/10 scale autonomous vehicle (teleoperated) retrofitted with advanced sensor and control technology, comprising a lower and upper level chassis and autonomous elements. The physical twin is engineered for adaptive cruise control (ACC) in the automotive domain, chosen for digital twinning due to its resource‑constrained ECUs, need for real‑time monitoring, and suitability for computation offloading to a cloud server.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system provides three main services: 1) Data Monitoring – real‑time display of vehicle state, projected trajectory, and detected object tracks via a UI that allows users to view, save, and generate reports of the data; 2) Computation Offloading – the ACC algorithm is offloaded from the vehicle to the DT (cloud), computing the desired speed from the lead‑vehicle distance and sending it back to the on‑board ECU for actuation; 3) Remote Management – operators can enable/disable ACC, set desired speed, and issue emergency braking commands through the UI, while developers use data collection and reporting for troubleshooting. These services cover monitoring, control, optimization (via offloading), and remote visualization.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin is implemented on a middleware infrastructure that supports low end‑to‑end latency communication between the vehicle and the DT, integrates data collection and reporting components, and respects the safety‑critical nature of the ACC system. The DT Entity, physical and middleware components, and UI constitute the digital twin system, leveraging cloud computing resources for ACC processing, a real‑time network layer for communication, and a UI framework capable of rendering live telemetry and management controls.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: Clock synchronization between physical and digital components is mandated to have an accuracy better than 100 ms, in line with many safety‑critical vehicular standards; computation offloading therefore requires a 100 ms synchronization period. The DT’s time granularity is a 100 ms tick, with ROS2 publish/subscribe exchanges timestamped to this resolution. Latency between sensor events on the physical twin and corresponding actuator commands in the digital twin is bounded at ≤100 ms, ensuring real‑time operation.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The described DT is a single instance composed of three entities: Physical Twin Entity, Digital Twin Entity, and User Entity. All entities are deployed on distinct hardware platforms, creating a decentralized distribution of functionality yet a centralized logical twin. No multiplicity of twin replicas is defined; coordination exists only within the single instance. Responsibilities are scoped per entity: the Physical Twin handles sensor interfaces and basic processing (point‑cloud clustering & tracking), the Digital Twin manages data storage, control logic, and analytics, while the User Entity provides visualization and control panels.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system follows an entity‑based architecture derived from ISO 23247 RA, mapping each functional element (FE) to individual ROS 2 packages that contain one or more ROS 2 nodes. The User Interface FE is split into 3D Visualization and Control Panel sub‑components. Data flows are managed by ROS 2 topics and services; each entity resides on its own compute node, and all nodes communicate via the ROS 2 DDS middleware. Resource management is implicit in this deployment: the Physical Twin Entity exposes sensor/actuator drivers, the Digital Twin Entity implements data storage and control algorithms, and the User Entity supplies UI. System‑wide coordination is achieved through ROS 2’s publish/subscribe paradigm combined with a shared time base (100 ms resolution).
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data flows from the physical twin to the digital twin begin with LiDAR sensing that generates raw point‑cloud data. This raw stream is processed by the Point Cloud Clustering & Tracking software component, which clusters spatial points and tracks object trajectories, converting the data into a structured 2‑D point‑cloud representation suitable for further use. The processed and sensor data streams are then handled by the ISO 23247 Data Translation Feature Engine (FE), which serializes the payloads, aligns disparate message types, and performs necessary unit conversions to ensure semantic consistency across the system. Communication between software components within the digital twin runs on a ROS 2 network using the DDS/RTPS middleware, while inter‑entity (physical ↔ digital) communication occurs over a Wi‑Fi network utilizing UDP as the transport layer. The physical twin’s vehicle state and object tracks are displayed in real‑time on a 3‑D visualization module, providing an immediate quality‑assurance view of the sensor data. Event triggers are driven by the LiDAR acquisition cycle, which initiates the clustering‑tracking pipeline, and by vehicle state changes that propagate through the Data Translation FE for integration into the digital twin’s state models.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands issued from the digital twin are routed through the Actuation Feature Engine, which handles motor actuation, steering servo actuation, and related actuation commands. These command messages are serialized and unit‑converted by the Data Translation FE before transmission over the Wi‑Fi UDP link to the physical twin’s actuators. Feedback on vehicle state and actuator pose is streamed back to the digital twin via the same Data Translation pipeline, allowing the digital twin to confirm command execution through updated state information. The document does not specify detailed validation, authentication, or conflict‑resolution mechanisms for these control messages.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The digital twin’s internal software components communicate over a ROS 2 network that implements the DDS/RTPS middleware for publish/subscribe messaging. Inter‑entity links between the physical twin and the digital twin utilize a Wi‑Fi wireless network with UDP transport. This hybrid architecture comprises a distributed ROS 2 bus for intra‑system messaging surrounded by a wireless UDP overlay for external connectivity. Specific bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, or security requirements are not detailed in the provided documentation.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system follows the ISO 23247 – Digital Twin Framework for Manufacturing standard (published 2021). ISO 23247 defines a four‑part framework: (1) Overview and general principles; (2) Reference architecture; (3) Digital representation of physical manufacturing elements; (4) Information exchange. The reference architecture is entity‑based, comprising four primary entities: (i) Observable Manufacturing Element (OME) – any item with observable physical presence or operation (equipment, facilities, products, personnel, processes); (ii) Data Collection and Device Control Entity – collects data from and controls the OME; (iii) Digital Twin Entity – the digital representation of the OME; and (iv) User Entity – provides interfaces for users to monitor data or control the OME. Each entity is composed of Functional Entities (FEs), which are modular units responsible for discrete tasks such as actuating the OME, transforming data into human‑interpretable formats, or interfacing with users. The standard does not prescribe specific implementations for FEs, allowing flexibility; for example, Functional Entities can be implemented using OPC Unified Architecture (OPC UA). The standard mandates compliance with ISO 23247 requirements, including the four-part structure and the entity‑based reference architecture, ensuring interoperability across manufacturing digital twin implementations.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: ISO 26262‑1 outlines a functional safety lifecycle covering hazard analysis, risk assessment, safety goal definition, safety requirements specification, safety verification, and safety validation, ensuring safety‑critical vehicle systems meet specified safety integrity levels. 3GPP TS 22.185 prescribes cybersecurity protocols for V2X services, including mutual authentication via 5G security context establishment, message encryption using AES‑128/256 in CCM mode, integrity protection, and a public key infrastructure (PKI) for non‑repudiation. It also specifies threat identification, vulnerability analysis, and contingency measures that enable fail‑safe mechanisms and comprehensive risk mitigation strategies.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
_A. Context_


DT technology enables an array of benefits for ACC through
off-loading any critical or non-critical computation for which
resource-constrained electronic control units (ECU) are typically not sufficient. To implement the ACC-DT, we develop
the following three services through a DT.
_1) Data Monitoring:_ Monitoring ACC-relevant data flowing through a vehicle is crucial for tracking system performance. The DT presented in this case will contain a user
interface (UI) allowing a user to view, save, and generate
reports of vehicle state data. Additionally, the UI will display
real-time vehicle state data, the projected driving path, and
detected object tracks in the vehicle’s proximity.
_2) Computation Offloading:_ A DT can alleviate the computational burden placed on resource-constrained on-board
hardware by allocating compute to better-suited equipment,
e.g., in the cloud. In this case study, we demonstrate this
functionality by offloading only the ACC system from the
vehicle to the DT. The ACC system determines the lead
vehicle, then computes a desired speed based on the distance
to the lead vehicle, finally sending the desired speed back to
the vehicle where the actuation is carried out.


(a) Base hardware: teleoperated vehicle retrofitted with advanced
sensor and control technology



(b) Main hardware layers of the physical twin: lower and upper level
chassis, and the autonomy elements



Fig. 1: The physical twin: a 1/10 [th] -scale autonomous vehicle



_3) Remote Management:_ The ability to remotely manage
ADAS systems enables the development, testing, and operation
of these systems from a different geographical location than
the physical twin. In addition to displaying real-time state data,
the UI will also enable the management and operation of ACC
remotely. This includes enabling/disabling ACC, setting the
desired speed, and sending an emergency braking command.
To implement these services, the DT must support low
end-to-end latency communication with the vehicle, contain
data collection and reporting capabilities, and more—all while
respecting the safety-critical nature of the system. For convenience and following community best practices, we refer
to the collection of the DT Entity, physical and middleware
components, and the UI as the digital twin system (DTS).
The users of our system can be grouped into two classes:
operators and developers. Operators use the DT to monitor
and remotely manage ACC, and developers use the DT to
troubleshoot during development using the data collection and
monitoring capabilities. In the _Liberty to act vs. Ability to act_
taxonomy from David and Bork [30], this DTS falls under the
Fully Autonomous Digital Twin, with the Human-in-the-Loop.


_B. Physical Twin_

coordination between devices. To enable computation
offloading, devices must share the same notion of time.
Many standards mandate stricter clock synchronization
than 100 ms for safety-critical vehicular applications. We
require 100 ms accuracy due to hardware limitations.


_B. Architecture_


We draw on the ISO 23247 RA in the architectural design
phase. First, we identify key software (SW) components
(Tab. I) and their relationships to each other, and to the sensors
and actuators of the physical twin (Fig. 2).
Next, we map the SW components onto FEs and implement
them. To enable their execution on the vehicle, we implement
each FE as an individual ROS2 package. Each package contains one or more ROS2 nodes implementing the functionality
provided by the FE. This mapping helps organize the SW
components by the entity-based structure of ISO 23247.
User-facing components are organized into the _User Entity_,
within which the singular _User Interface FE_ is instantiated
in the _3D Visualization_ and _Control Panel_ components. SW
components that implement data management and control
mechanisms are organized into the _Digital Twin Entity_ . Data
pre-processing, actuation, and parts of the device communication functionality are organized into the _Physical Twin_
_Entity_ . The existence of this entity marks a departure from
the ISO 23247 RA. Most similar to the Physical Twin Entity
in the standard is the OME, which hosts resource-specific
functionality. By contrast, in our design, the Physical Twin
Entity reflects the inherent resources of the twinned system (e.g., compute, sensors, actuators) and can host general
automotive functionality, such as _Point Cloud Clustering &_
_Tracking_ . Considering that each entity has been deployed on
a different hardware component, such deviations from the
standard are substantial as they may impact the ownership of
the functionality and the engineering process of the DTS.
Not every ISO 23247 FE was required for our DTS. At the
same time, the _User Interface FE_ has been split into two subcomponents: visualization and control panel. Finally, one SW
component, _Data Storage_ did not have a naturally aligned FE.


**Physical Twin Entity*** **Digital Twin Entity** **User Entity**

In addition, a demonstration of the DT is publicly available. [2]


II. B ACKGROUND AND RELATED WORK


_A. The ISO 23247 standard and reference architecture_


In 2021, the International Standards Organization (ISO)
published the _ISO 23247 – Digital twin framework for manu-_
_facturing_ standard [10] that defines a development framework
for DTs. The standard is organized into four parts: 1) Overview
and general principles, 2) Reference architecture, 3) Digital
representation of physical manufacturing elements, and 4)
Information exchange. For our purposes, Part 2 is the most
relevant as it defines an entity-based RA, with each entity is
responsible for implementing a part of the DT’s functionality.
The _Observable Manufacturing Element (OME)_ is any item
that has an observable physical presence or operation in
manufacturing, e.g., equipment, facilities, products, personnel,
processes, etc. The _Data Collection and Device Control Entity_
is responsible for collecting data from, and controlling the
OME. The _Digital Twin Entity_ digitally represents the OME.
Finally, the _User Entity_ provides interfaces for users to interact
with the _Digital Twin Entity_, e.g., monitoring data or controlling the OME. Each entity is composed of _Functional Entities_
(FEs), which are smaller units responsible for a particular task
such as actuating the OME, transforming data into a humaninterpretable format, or interfacing with the user. The standard
does not give guidelines as to how FEs are to be implemented.
For example, one can choose to use OPC Unified Architecture

Implementation of Services_ Below, we describe each DTS service as a composition of multiple SW components, summarized in Tab. II. All of the DTS services rely on the Data Translation component, which corresponds to the ISO 23247 Data Translation FE. This component serializes data, aligns message types, and performs unit conversions for every data stream in the system.
